label: verify-7-cal-5th-372
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- 7 Cal.5th 372

## How the documents used it

[delphi] … four of five flagged "verify")
- *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 — 4 (two flagged verification of pinpoint applicability)
- *Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District* (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 — 3
- *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — 2

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130
- *City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist.* (2011) 198 Cal.App…

[dialectic] …igation case law, e.g., the *Bighorn*/*Plantier* line, that agencies — not challengers — must affirmatively prove cost-of-service compliance once a fee is challenged).
- *Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water Dist.* (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 confirms that a facial/methodological challenge to a fee's compliance with § 6(b) can proceed without parcel-by-parcel individualized proof from the plaintiff — the ratepayer association can attack the rate study's meth…

[steelman] …e § 53759's 120-day limitations period, and research current case law on refund vs. prospective-reformation remedies for partially deficient Prop 218 fees (see generally *Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water Dist.* (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 on procedural vehicle, which may affect available remedy).
…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q2 — Drought surcharge funding low-income rebates (Prop 218).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"MISCHARACTERIZED","canonical_cite":"Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372","note":"The case is real and the citation form is correct, but Plantier's actual holding is narrow: a Prop. 218 challenge to an agency's *method* of allocating a fee (as opposed to the rate itself) need not exhaust administrative remedies via the § 6(a) protest hearing, because that hearing is an inadequate forum to evaluate an established allocation methodology. It is not a burden-of-proof/evidentiary case. The [dialectic] snippet's use — that Plantier \"confirms\" a facial/methodological challenge \"can proceed without parcel-by-parcel individualized proof from the plaintiff\" as part of a \"Bighorn/Plantier line\" requiring agencies to affirmatively prove cost-of-service compliance — conflates Plantier's exhaustion-of-remedies holding with an evidentiary burden-shifting rule the case doesn't address (that burden-shifting rule actually derives from Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(5) itself, and Bighorn addressed a different issue — initiative power over rates, not proof burdens). The [steelman] snippet's hedged reference to Plantier \"on procedural vehicle, which may affect available remedy\" is a fair characterization; the [delphi] mention is a bare citation-count listing with no substantive claim attached.","source_urls":["https://law.justia.com/cases/california/supreme-court/2019/s243360.html","https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/plantier-v-ramona-mun-water-dist-34614","https://www.counties.org/post/plantier-v-ramona-municipal-water-district-s243360","https://kmtg.com/news/legal-alerts/proposition-218-hearing-may-not-be-required-to-challenge-method-for-applying-rates-for-property-related-service/"]}

data.canonical_cite: Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372
data.note: The case is real and the citation form is correct, but Plantier's actual holding is narrow: a Prop. 218 challenge to an agency's *method* of allocating a fee (as opposed to the rate itself) need not exhaust administrative remedies via the § 6(a) protest hearing, because that hearing is an inadequate forum to evaluate an established allocation methodology. It is not a burden-of-proof/evidentiary case. The [dialectic] snippet's use — that Plantier "confirms" a facial/methodological challenge "can proceed without parcel-by-parcel individualized proof from the plaintiff" as part of a "Bighorn/Plantier line" requiring agencies to affirmatively prove cost-of-service compliance — conflates Plantier's exhaustion-of-remedies holding with an evidentiary burden-shifting rule the case doesn't address (that burden-shifting rule actually derives from Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(5) itself, and Bighorn addressed a different issue — initiative power over rates, not proof burdens). The [steelman] snippet's hedged reference to Plantier "on procedural vehicle, which may affect available remedy" is a fair characterization; the [delphi] mention is a bare citation-count listing with no substantive claim attached.
